template_name: Standard Prescription Format - Prolonged Unconjugated Jaundice

condition.name: Prolonged Unconjugated Jaundice
overview: Jaundice beyond 14 days in a term neonate or beyond 21 days in a preterm infant warrants evaluation.
If the conjugated fraction is normal, the jaundice is unconjugated and is often benign, but it may indicate underlying pathology.
The first step is to distinguish unconjugated from conjugated hyperbilirubinemia by fractionated bilirubin.

definition.practical_pearl: Do not label prolonged jaundice as benign until direct bilirubin is checked and conjugated jaundice is excluded.

frequent_benign_or_common: Hemolysis (ABO incompatibility, Rh incompatibility, G6PD deficiency)
less_common_or_pathological: Occult infection such as urinary tract infection

history_template: Age at onset and duration of jaundice
History of poor feeding or dehydration
Family history of jaundice or hemolytic disorders
Any sibling with kernicterus or neonatal death
Drug exposure or hemolysis triggers if relevant
examination_template: General appearance, activity, tone and growth
Signs of birth injury such as cephalhematoma
Signs of congenital hypothyroidism including macroglossia and large fontanel
Neurological status including alertness and reflexes
Overall thriving versus sick baby appearance

first_line_investigations.name: Total bilirubin and Direct bilirubin
first_line_investigations.purpose: Confirm that jaundice is unconjugated and exclude cholestasis

optional_investigations.name: Serum albumin and LFTs
optional_investigations.indication: If baby is unwell or liver disease suspected

optional_investigations.name: Urine reducing substances
optional_investigations.indication: If galactosemia or metabolic disease is suspected

diagnosis_by_pattern.probable_diagnosis: G6PD deficiency, Gilbert syndrome, or Crigler-Najjar syndrome

general_principles: Continue breastfeeding unless a specific contraindication exists
Treat the underlying cause when identified
Follow-up with clinical and bilirubin monitoring

management: Avoid hemolysis triggers in G6PD deficiency

cause_specific_management.condition: Unwell baby or suspicion of cholestasis
management: Do not manage as simple breast milk jaundice

parent_counseling: Some jaundice after 2 weeks can be common, especially in breastfed babies. — 2 हफ्ते बाद भी पीलिया हो सकता है, खासकर माँ का दूध पीने वाले बच्चों में।
Breastfeeding is usually safe and should generally be continued. — माँ का दूध सुरक्षित है और आमतौर पर जारी रखना चाहिए।
Further tests are needed mainly to rule out uncommon but important causes. — जांच कुछ दुर्लभ लेकिन गंभीर कारणों को दूर करने के लिए ज़रूरी है।
Parents should monitor feeding, urine color, stool color, activity and weight gain. — दूध पीना, पेशाब का रंग, पोटी का रंग, हिलना-डुलना और वज़न बढ़ना ध्यान से देखें।
Follow-up is important until jaundice resolves. — पीलिया ठीक होने तक नियमित जांच कराएं।
flow_logic: If jaundice persists beyond 14 days in a term baby or 21 days in a preterm baby, measure total and direct bilirubin
If direct bilirubin is elevated, evaluate for cholestasis
If conjugated fraction is normal, assess feeding, growth, urine and stool color
If baby is well, stools are normal and breastfeeding is exclusive, breast milk jaundice is likely
Still run TSH, CBC, reticulocyte count, blood group, Coombs and G6PD to rule out treatable causes
Continue follow-up with clinical and bilirubin monitoring
quick_checklist_for_opd: Duration more than 14 days in term or more than 21 days in preterm?
Have hemolysis and hypothyroidism been evaluated?
Are stool and urine colors normal?
Have parents been counseled and follow-up planned?

standard_prescription_format.title: Standard Prescription - Prolonged Unconjugated Jaundice
chief_complaints: Yellow discoloration persisting beyond expected period
provisional_diagnosis_options: Prolonged unconjugated jaundice - likely breast milk jaundice
Prolonged jaundice - rule out congenital hypothyroidism

if_hemolysis_suspected: Avoid triggers if G6PD deficiency suspected or confirmed
if_direct_bilirubin_high_or_baby_unwell: Urgent referral for cholestasis / liver work-up

warning_signs: Dark urine — गहरे रंग का पेशाब
Pale or white stools — सफ़ेद या हल्के रंग की पोटी
Poor feeding or lethargy — दूध न पीना या सुस्ती
Fever or illness — बुखार या बीमारी
Bleeding from any site — कहीं से खून आना
expected_course: Breast milk jaundice (the most common cause) is benign and self-limiting, typically resolving by 3-4 months of age. Breastfeeding should be continued. However, treatable conditions like congenital hypothyroidism, hemolysis, and cholestasis must be ruled out before labeling jaundice as breast milk jaundice.
key_clinical_points: Always fractionate bilirubin (total + direct) before attributing jaundice to breast milk.
TSH and Free T4 must be checked to rule out congenital hypothyroidism.
Reticulocyte count, blood group, Coombs test, and G6PD screen for hemolysis.
Normal stool and urine color with a thriving baby suggests breast milk jaundice.
Never label jaundice as benign until conjugated bilirubin is confirmed normal.

severity_assessment.mild: Thriving baby with unconjugated jaundice, normal stools, normal feeding — likely breast milk jaundice, monitor.
severity_assessment.moderate: Unconjugated jaundice with borderline weight gain or mildly elevated reticulocytes — complete hemolysis and thyroid workup.
severity_assessment.severe: Elevated direct bilirubin, pale stools, dark urine, sick baby, hepatosplenomegaly — urgent cholestasis/liver workup and referral.
